main_paper.raw: {"methods":["MILCA (Multiple Instance Learning using Counting and Attention): a method that counts/sums selected features associated with a class, optionally weights features using a fully connected network, and replaces softmax with a projection to [-1,1] or [0,1] for feature coefficients, allowing counting rather than just attention."],"problems":["Improving accuracy and efficiency in Multiple Instance Learning (MIL) classification tasks, especially when the number of features is large compared to the number of bags.","Addressing the limitations of current Embedded Space (ES) MIL solutions, which may be overly complex and not necessarily more accurate than simpler methods."],"datasets":["Multiple previous and new real-world MIL tasks (specific datasets not named in the provided text)","Sequenced T Cell Receptor Repertoires for recovering host disease history"],"metrics":["Accuracy"],"results":[{"metric":"Accuracy improvement over SOTA","value":"3% higher accuracy than current state-of-the-art on average"}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes a simple counting/summing algorithm for MIL that can outperform or match the accuracy of more complex current solutions.","Introduces a method to weight features using a fully connected network and replaces softmax with a projection to [-1,1] or [0,1], allowing the model to count features rather than just attend to them.","Demonstrates that in many MIL classification tasks, a weighted sum of informative features is as effective or better than complex models, especially when the number of features is large compared to the number of bags.","Shows significant efficiency gains and accuracy improvements over existing MIL algorithms."]}

methods: MILCA (Multiple Instance Learning using Counting and Attention): a method that counts/sums selected features associated with a class, optionally weights features using a fully connected network, and replaces softmax with a projection to [-1,1] or [0,1] for feature coefficients, allowing counting rather than just attention.
problems: Improving accuracy and efficiency in Multiple Instance Learning (MIL) classification tasks, especially when the number of features is large compared to the number of bags.
Addressing the limitations of current Embedded Space (ES) MIL solutions, which may be overly complex and not necessarily more accurate than simpler methods.
datasets: Multiple previous and new real-world MIL tasks (specific datasets not named in the provided text)
Sequenced T Cell Receptor Repertoires for recovering host disease history

results.value: 3% higher accuracy than current state-of-the-art on average

novelty_claims: Proposes a simple counting/summing algorithm for MIL that can outperform or match the accuracy of more complex current solutions.
Introduces a method to weight features using a fully connected network and replaces softmax with a projection to [-1,1] or [0,1], allowing the model to count features rather than just attend to them.

Demonstrates that in many MIL classification tasks, a weighted sum of informative features is as effective or better than complex models, especially when the number of features is large compared to the number of bags.
Shows significant efficiency gains and accuracy improvements over existing MIL algorithms.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Counting method for TCR repertoire classification","Attention model (attTCR) using Variational AutoEncoder (VAE) projections to weight TCRs","Graph Convolutional Network approach (gTCR) using TCR repertoire co-occurrence graphs"],"problems":["Classifying T-cell receptor (TCR) repertoires to detect disease history (e.g., CMV infection) and HLA alleles","Addressing the challenges of low overlap, non-injectivity, large diversity, and extremely low witness rate in TCR repertoire data","Improving accuracy of immune repertoire-based disease detection beyond current machine learning and Bayesian methods"],"datasets":["Large CMV binding dataset used by multiple groups (including references 8, 13, 16, 17)"],"metrics":["Classification accuracy"],"results":[{"metric":"Classification accuracy (CMV and HLA prediction)","value":"Counting and attention algorithms outperform all existing algorithms; gTCR achieves accuracy comparable to attention model"}],"novelty_claims":["Demonstrates that a simple counting method outperforms state-of-the-art machine learning and Bayesian methods for TCR repertoire classification","Introduces a novel attention model (attTCR) based on VAE projections to further improve performance by weighting TCRs","Proposes a new Graph Convolutional Network approach (gTCR) that balances the accuracy of the attention model with the simplicity of the counting model"]}

methods: Counting method for TCR repertoire classification
Attention model (attTCR) using Variational AutoEncoder (VAE) projections to weight TCRs
Graph Convolutional Network approach (gTCR) using TCR repertoire co-occurrence graphs
problems: Classifying T-cell receptor (TCR) repertoires to detect disease history (e.g., CMV infection) and HLA alleles
Addressing the challenges of low overlap, non-injectivity, large diversity, and extremely low witness rate in TCR repertoire data
Improving accuracy of immune repertoire-based disease detection beyond current machine learning and Bayesian methods
datasets: Large CMV binding dataset used by multiple groups (including references 8, 13, 16, 17)

results.value: Counting and attention algorithms outperform all existing algorithms; gTCR achieves accuracy comparable to attention model

novelty_claims: Demonstrates that a simple counting method outperforms state-of-the-art machine learning and Bayesian methods for TCR repertoire classification
Introduces a novel attention model (attTCR) based on VAE projections to further improve performance by weighting TCRs
Proposes a new Graph Convolutional Network approach (gTCR) that balances the accuracy of the attention model with the simplicity of the counting model

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Attention-based deep multiple instance learning (MIL) model","Permutation-invariant aggregation operator using attention mechanism","Parameterization of all transformations (instance embedding, aggregation, bag probability) with neural networks (convolutional and fully-connected layers)","Trainable weighted average aggregation (attention mechanism) instead of max or mean operators","End-to-end training by optimizing the log-likelihood of the Bernoulli bag label"],"problems":["Improving flexibility and interpretability in multiple instance learning (MIL)","Learning bag-level labels from sets of instances in a permutation-invariant way","Identifying key instances contributing to the bag label in MIL"],"datasets":["Benchmark MIL datasets (not specified by name)","MNIST-based MIL dataset","Two real-life histopathology image datasets"],"metrics":["Classification accuracy","Interpretability (ability to identify key instances)"],"results":[{"metric":"Classification accuracy (MNIST-based MIL dataset)","value":"Outperforms other methods"},{"metric":"Classification accuracy (histopathology datasets)","value":"Outperforms other methods"},{"metric":"Classification accuracy (benchmark MIL datasets)","value":"Comparable to best MIL methods"}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes a neural network-based permutation-invariant aggregation operator corresponding to the attention mechanism for MIL","Parameterizes all steps of the MIL model (embedding, aggregation, bag probability) with neural networks for increased flexibility and end-to-end training","Introduces a trainable weighted average (attention) aggregation instead of traditional max or mean operators","Provides interpretability by allowing identification of key instances contributing to the bag label via attention weights"]}

problems: Improving flexibility and interpretability in multiple instance learning (MIL)

Bag augmentation method for classifier phase
problems: High computational cost and memory consumption in Whole Slide Image (WSI) classification using Multiple Instance Learning (MIL)

novelty_claims: Proposes ICMIL, an iterative coupling of the bag classifier and patch embedder to mitigate domain shift and semantic gap in MIL for WSI classification.
Introduces a confidence-based teacher-student framework for effective and robust knowledge transfer from bag-level classifier to instance-level patch embedder, enabling weakly-supervised fine-tuning.
Presents a novel bag augmentation method for the classifier phase.
Demonstrates that a bag-level classifier can serve as a good instance-level teacher, improving instance representation learning.
Provides extensive experimental validation on four datasets, showing state-of-the-art performance and significant improvements over existing MIL backbones.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Aggregated Informativeness (AGIN): assesses informativeness of each instance based on classifier uncertainty and aggregates to compute bag informativeness, prioritizing instances near decision boundaries.","Cluster-Based Aggregative Sampling (C-BAS): clusters data hierarchically in the instance space and computes a criterion based on cluster composition knowledge and conflict between bag and instance labels, promoting exploration and discouraging queries where labels can be inferred from bag labels."],"problems":["Active learning for instance classification in multiple instance learning (MIL) settings, where data are arranged in bags with weak labels.","Reducing annotation costs in instance classification problems with weak supervision.","Selecting the most informative bags to query in MIL for efficient instance label discovery."],"datasets":["Benchmark datasets from text classification, image classification, and sound classification domains (exact dataset names not specified in the provided content)."],"metrics":["Number of queries needed to achieve a given performance level (label efficiency).","Classifier performance (e.g., accuracy, though the specific metric is not named in the provided content)."],"results":[{"metric":"Number of queries needed to achieve target performance","value":"Significantly reduced compared to reference (single-instance AL) methods"},{"metric":"Classifier performance","value":"Proposed methods outperform reference methods in extensive experiments"}],"novelty_claims":["First to address the MIAL scenario where the learner predicts instance labels by querying bags, not previously studied in the literature.","Proposes two new bag-level aggregation methods (AGIN and C-BAS) for selecting informative bags in MIAL instance classification.","Demonstrates that appropriate bag-level strategies in MIAL can significantly reduce the number of queries needed compared to single-instance AL methods."]}

methods: Aggregated Informativeness (AGIN): assesses informativeness of each instance based on classifier uncertainty and aggregates to compute bag informativeness, prioritizing instances near decision boundaries.

Cluster-Based Aggregative Sampling (C-BAS): clusters data hierarchically in the instance space and computes a criterion based on cluster composition knowledge and conflict between bag and instance labels, promoting exploration and discouraging queries where labels can be inferred from bag labels.
problems: Active learning for instance classification in multiple instance learning (MIL) settings, where data are arranged in bags with weak labels.
Reducing annotation costs in instance classification problems with weak supervision.
Selecting the most informative bags to query in MIL for efficient instance label discovery.
datasets: Benchmark datasets from text classification, image classification, and sound classification domains (exact dataset names not specified in the provided content).
metrics: Number of queries needed to achieve a given performance level (label efficiency).
Classifier performance (e.g., accuracy, though the specific metric is not named in the provided content).

results.metric: Number of queries needed to achieve target performance
results.value: Significantly reduced compared to reference (single-instance AL) methods
Proposed methods outperform reference methods in extensive experiments

novelty_claims: First to address the MIAL scenario where the learner predicts instance labels by querying bags, not previously studied in the literature.
Proposes two new bag-level aggregation methods (AGIN and C-BAS) for selecting informative bags in MIAL instance classification.
Demonstrates that appropriate bag-level strategies in MIAL can significantly reduce the number of queries needed compared to single-instance AL methods.

problems: Patch-level classification of breast tumour histology images with limited and weak (image-level) annotations

Handling variability in staining, acquisition, and annotation in medical image datasets
datasets: Microscopy images from different patients and acquisition methods

novelty_claims: Integration of Bag of Visual Words (BoVW) as a feature selector with deep CNN features in an end-to-end patch classification pipeline for medical images
Demonstration that combining deep learning with classical feature engineering improves performance on small, complex, multi-class histology datasets
No need for heavy preprocessing (e.g., stain/color normalization, data augmentation) or post-processing of patch predictions
Flexibility in choice of pre-trained CNN backbone for feature extraction
Robustness demonstrated across different datasets (WSI and microscopy images) and resolutions

Domain-invariant Feature Adaptation (DFA) module using Maximum Mean Discrepancy (MMD)
Cross-domain Vanishing Bridge (CVB) module for removing domain-specific information

problems: Unsupervised domain adaptation for crowd counting

Reducing the need for expensive and time-consuming annotation of new datasets
novelty_claims: Proposes a cross-domain learning network for unsupervised domain adaptation in crowd counting
Introduces a Multi-granularity Feature-aware Discriminator (MFD) module that combines global and local feature alignment
Incorporates a Domain-invariant Feature Adaptation (DFA) module that explicitly aligns feature distributions using Maximum Mean Discrepancy (MMD)
Presents a Cross-domain Vanishing Bridge (CVB) module to incrementally remove domain-specific information from features
Demonstrates competitive performance to state-of-the-art methods using several backbone networks

methods: FusionCount architecture: adaptive fusion of a large majority of encoded features for multiscale feature extraction
Channel reduction block for extracting saliency information during decoding
problems: Efficient and accurate crowd counting from images
Handling scale variation in crowd images due to perspective distortion
Reducing computational cost in multiscale feature extraction for crowd counting
novelty_claims: Proposes a novel crowd counting architecture (FusionCount) that adaptively fuses most encoded features to obtain multiscale features, avoiding extra extraction modules.
Covers a more extensive range of receptive field sizes (6 to 192) than previous methods.
Introduces a new channel reduction block that extracts saliency information during decoding.
Achieves state-of-the-art or comparable results on benchmark datasets with significantly reduced computational complexity.

methods: Global Attention Mechanism (GAM)
3D-permutation with multilayer-perceptron for channel attention
Convolutional spatial attention submodule
problems: Loss of information in channel and spatial attention mechanisms due to dimension separation
Insufficient cross-dimension (channel-spatial) interactions in existing attention mechanisms
Improving performance of deep neural networks for image classification
novelty_claims: Proposes a global attention mechanism (GAM) that retains information to enhance channel-spatial interactions.
Introduces a 3D-permutation with multilayer-perceptron for channel attention.
Combines channel and spatial attention in a way that magnifies global interactive representations and reduces information reduction.
Demonstrates stable outperformance over several recent attention mechanisms on standard benchmarks with both ResNet and MobileNet architectures.

methods: MotifBoost (k-mer based classification method exploiting short motifs of TCRs)
Comparison with deep learning methods for repertoire classification
problems: Efficient and robust classification of T cell receptor (TCR) repertoires from next-generation sequencing data
Performance degradation of existing machine learning methods on small sample sizes in immune repertoire classification
novelty_claims: Proposes MotifBoost, a k-mer (short motif) based method for immune repertoire classification that is data-efficient and robust to small sample sizes
Demonstrates that MotifBoost performs as efficiently as deep learning methods on large datasets and is more stable and reliable on small datasets
Clarifies that motif-based features provide robust representation for repertoire classification
Shows that sequence identity and sequence motifs encode partially different information, suggesting the need for combining complementary information for improved repertoire analysis

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Similarity-Aware Feature Enhancement block (SAFECount)","Similarity Comparison Module (SCM)","Feature Enhancement Module (FEM)"],"problems":["Few-shot object counting, where the goal is to count the number of exemplar objects (described by support images) in a query image, especially when objects are densely packed","Generalization to novel object classes not seen during training"],"datasets":["FSC-147","CARPK"],"metrics":["Mean Absolute Error (MAE)"],"results":[{"metric":"Mean Absolute Error (MAE) on FSC-147","value":"14.32"},{"metric":"Improvement over previous SOTA MAE on FSC-147","value":"from 22.08 to 14.32 (35% improvement)"}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes a novel Similarity-Aware Feature Enhancement block (SAFECount) for few-shot object counting, integrating both similarity and feature information for improved performance.","Introduces a Similarity Comparison Module (SCM) that learns feature projections and compares projected features to derive reliable similarity maps.","Develops a Feature Enhancement Module (FEM) that uses point-wise similarities to fuse support features into the query feature, focusing on regions similar to the exemplar object.","Demonstrates substantial improvement over state-of-the-art methods, especially in densely packed object scenarios."]}

methods: Similarity-Aware Feature Enhancement block (SAFECount)
Similarity Comparison Module (SCM)
Feature Enhancement Module (FEM)
problems: Few-shot object counting, where the goal is to count the number of exemplar objects (described by support images) in a query image, especially when objects are densely packed
Generalization to novel object classes not seen during training

results.metric: Improvement over previous SOTA MAE on FSC-147

novelty_claims: Proposes a novel Similarity-Aware Feature Enhancement block (SAFECount) for few-shot object counting, integrating both similarity and feature information for improved performance.
Introduces a Similarity Comparison Module (SCM) that learns feature projections and compares projected features to derive reliable similarity maps.
Develops a Feature Enhancement Module (FEM) that uses point-wise similarities to fuse support features into the query feature, focusing on regions similar to the exemplar object.
Demonstrates substantial improvement over state-of-the-art methods, especially in densely packed object scenarios.
